sections.paragraph:
prop. a river, so called from its bursting forth from its fountains, compare Job 40:23. To this answers the Arab. جَيْحَانُ and جَيْحُونُ, which the Arabs commonly use of larger rivers, as the Ganges, Araxes. In Hebrew it is pr.n.-

(1) Of a fountain, with a stream and ponds, near Jerusalem, called elsewhere שִׁלֹחַ [But this is a mistake, they were different], 1Ki 1:33, 38 1Ki 1:38 2Ch 32:30 33:14 .

(2) The second of the four rivers of Paradise, which is said to surround the land of כּוּשׁ (Ethiopia), Gen 2:13. Some who follow the Arabic use of the word جيحون understand the Araxes, and they take כּוּשׁ in this place in a signification entirely different from that which it commonly has; but this is improbable. On the other hand it was the constant opinion of the ancients that the Nile was intended; see Jer 2:18 LXX. Sir_24:27 Joseph. Arch. i. 1, § 3; and I expect that the Ethiopic Nile was particularly meant, which may in fact be said to surround Ethiopia. I have discussed this more at length in Thesaur. pages 281, 282.
